description: Quality evals for READMEs generated by skill-readme-writer. Each eval verifies that a generated README conforms to its type-specific strategy AND the 7 universal iron laws.

evals.prompt: Write a README for guizang-ppt-skill, a PPT generation skill that produces HTML slide decks with WebGL backgrounds
evals.expected_output: A visual-first README where screenshots/demos dominate the first screen, effect proof appears before install command
assertions: Has a hook line that names what it produces in the first 3 lines
Effect proof (screenshots, GIF descriptions, or visual anchors) appears BEFORE the install command
Install command is visible in the first screen — no scrolling required
Shows at least 1 style variation or output mode preview
Includes a Quick Use / first use section showing what the user sends to their agent
Has at least 1 real conversation example (User: X / Agent: Y), not code snippets
States limitations honestly — what this skill cannot produce
Does not use Install/Usage/Contributing as the primary section structure
Does not contain placeholder text like {{PROJECT_NAME}}, TODO, or [insert screenshot]

evals.prompt: Write a README for caveman-skill, a skill that reduces conversation tokens by ~75% while keeping technical precision
evals.expected_output: A README with Before/After comparison that instantly shows the value proposition — less words, same meaning
assertions: Has a pain point hook that attacks the specific problem (verbose AI responses) within the first 5 lines
Includes at least 1 Before/After comparison — side by side or sequential, not just description
The Before/After comparison demonstrates quantifiable improvement (e.g., token count, word count, time saved)
Install command appears in the first scroll range
Includes a trust system or limitations section (what this skill does NOT do)
Has at least 1 real conversation example showing trigger words and agent response
Uses at least one Form of Before/After (visual diff, numbers table, competitor comparison, or conversation comparison)
Does not claim universal applicability — states when NOT to use
Does not contain placeholder text like TODO or [add example]

evals.prompt: Write a README for humanizer, a skill that detects and removes 24 patterns of AI-generated writing
evals.expected_output: A capability-focused README with a pattern checklist that lets readers scan what the skill can detect
assertions: Has a pattern checklist or capability inventory that is scannable (not buried in paragraphs)
Shows Before/After examples for at least 3 patterns — not just listing them
States the methodology source (where the patterns come from, who validated them)
Includes evidence of effectiveness — numbers, citations, or real dataset references
Has a 'When NOT to use' or limitations section that is specific, not generic
Install command is visible without scrolling
Has at least 1 conversation example showing the skill in action
Does not present the capability list as a wall of bullet points without context
Does not claim to detect things it cannot detect — stated limitations match actual capability

evals.prompt: Write a README for a Steve Jobs perspective skill that makes agents think and speak like Jobs
evals.expected_output: A README with 3+ comparison examples showing the style transformation, and a clear style anchor
assertions: Has a style anchor in the first screen — a quote, a character sketch, or a vivid description of the voice
Includes at least 3 comparison examples (same input → different outputs with/without the style)
Lists trigger words clearly — what the user says to activate the style
Has a 'When NOT to use' section that is specific to the style's blind spots
Shows underlying methodology — what sources the style is built from
Install command is visible without scrolling
Has at least 1 conversation example showing the voice in dialogue
Does not just describe the style — demonstrates it through examples
Does not claim the style works for every situation

evals.prompt: Write a README for nuwa-skill, a collection of 20+ character perspective skills (Elon Musk, Steve Jobs, Feynman, etc.)
evals.expected_output: A collection README with clear quality standards, classification system, and a selection guide
assertions: States the quality bar — what qualifies a skill to be in this collection
Has a classification system grouping skills by domain or use case, not just an alphabetical list
Includes a selection guide — 'if you need X, use Y' type recommendations
States how many skills are in the collection
Explains the entry point distinction — how a user starts using it
Has honest gaps — what this collection does NOT cover or do poorly
Install command is visible without scrolling
Does not just dump a list of skill names without curation signals
Does not claim comprehensiveness — admits what's missing

evals.prompt: Write a README for github-skill, a platform tool that interacts with GitHub via the gh CLI
evals.expected_output: A platform README with one-command quickstart, progressive disclosure of capabilities, and clear prerequisites
assertions: Has a one-command quickstart — a single line the user can copy-paste to get started
States prerequisites before the quickstart (what must be installed first)
Uses progressive disclosure — basic operations first, advanced features later
Has a capability catalog organized by operation type (PRs, Issues, API, etc.)
Has a platform boundary section — what this skill CANNOT do (e.g., no write access without auth)
Install command is visible without scrolling
Has at least 1 conversation example showing real agent interaction
Does not hide prerequisites or assume the user has everything set up
Does not over-promise platform capabilities that don't exist

evals.prompt: Write a README for skill-vetter, a security auditing protocol for agent skills before installation
evals.expected_output: A security-focused README with a red flag checklist, risk classification tiers, and verdict system
assertions: Has a red flag or warning checklist — the reader can scan for what makes a skill dangerous
Includes a risk classification system with clear tiers (not just 'safe' vs 'unsafe')
Has a verdict system or decision framework — the reader knows what action to take
States domain authority — who built this, what credentials back it up
Has an explicit disclaimer that it does not replace professional security audit
Uses verb-driven descriptions for actions (scan, detect, warn, block) not abstract nouns
Install command is visible without scrolling
Has at least 1 conversation example showing a real audit scenario
Does not claim to catch everything — states known blind spots

evals.prompt: Write a README for model-validation, a 5-layer protocol for detecting overfitting in prediction models
evals.expected_output: A science-focused README with a multi-step workflow, red flags checklist, and visual aids showing healthy vs overfit patterns
assertions: Shows the complete multi-step workflow — the reader can see the entire protocol before diving in
Has a red flags or warning signs checklist — what indicates a problem at each step
Includes at least 1 visual aid or diagram description (e.g., learning curve, confusion matrix pattern)
States limitations — what the protocol CANNOT guarantee or detect
References methodology or academic foundations — where the protocol comes from
Install command is visible without scrolling
Has at least 1 conversation example showing the protocol in action
Does not present complex methodology as a black box — explains enough to build trust
Does not claim the protocol eliminates all risk — admits residual uncertainty

universal_checks: Install command is visible in the first scroll range
Has at least 1 real conversation example (User: X / Agent: Y)
States limitations honestly — lists what the skill CANNOT do
Does not use Install/Usage/Contributing as the primary section structure
Does not contain placeholder text like {{PROJECT_NAME}}, TODO, or [add example]
Has a hook that answers 'should I install?' within the first 10 seconds of reading
The effect display format matches the skill type (screenshots for Output, Before/After for Efficiency, etc.)
